name: Financial Ledger
description: SCOPE DECOMPOSITION. Tally is split into LEDGER (canonical accounts, transactions, spend categories), CLASSIFY (automatic suggestions and correction feedback), INGEST (PDF/CSV/Excel parsing and import review), BUDGET (monthly category amounts), INSIGHTS (daily/weekly/monthly analysis), and CHANNELS (optional web or messaging delivery). LEDGER is framed first because every downstream result depends on its financial facts.

ROOT PROBLEM AND JOURNEY. Dirk's spend is fragmented across three accounts at two banks. Budget and insight requests are symptoms: they cannot be trustworthy until account, transaction, transfer, and category facts have one precise, durable, agent-operable source. The 5 Whys chain is captured in the problem statement. Without a solution, consolidation remains delayed and manual, transfers or duplicates can distort spend, and category corrections cannot improve later analysis. The user journey is agent-first: discover a command and its schema, register an account, record or query a transaction, assign or correct a category, and receive a structured result that downstream modules can consume.

RECOMMENDED APPROACH A - LOCAL-FIRST CLI APPLICATION CORE. One self-contained Tally executable runs commands in-process against one embedded local datastore. The CLI is the stable public boundary: self-documenting help and schemas, JSON input/output, structured command results, deterministic exit codes, and version-matched skills. Reuse the intent of CORE vertical-slice, explicit-DI, and CommandResult prior art while deliberately rejecting Actions-specific HTTP endpoints, Aspire, PostgreSQL, RLS, multi-tenancy, and frontend assumptions. Web and WhatsApp are not selected now; an AI host or Hermes-like adapter invokes the CLI, and CHANNELS may later add a human surface. Complexity M; fits the budget; root-problem completeness 9/10; strongly builds on selected CORE conventions. Biggest risk: an under-specified embedded schema, migration, backup, or CLI compatibility policy could make a locally simple tool unreliable over years of financial history. Stress test: this approach fails if simultaneous channels, scheduled jobs, or remote access demonstrably require an always-on multi-client service; that evidence triggers a later ADR rather than speculative infrastructure now.

ALTERNATIVE B - SERVICE-FIRST SINGLE BINARY. The executable supports a persistent local service mode and the CLI is a thin client; web, messaging, and schedulers call the same service API. Complexity H; exceeds the initial one-process/no-daemon budget; root-problem completeness 10/10; builds less directly on the requested CLI-only operating model. Pros: natural concurrency and future channels. Cons: service lifecycle, local authentication, ports, upgrades, availability, and operational support before any ledger value exists. Biggest risk: infrastructure and security work dominate the finance domain. Rejected for the first release, but it remains an evolution path if Approach A's stress condition becomes true.

ALTERNATIVE C - DO LESS BY ADAPTING AN EXISTING FINANCE CLI. Store the ledger in an established personal-finance tool and build only Tally command aliases, normalized JSON projections, and agent skills. Complexity L; fits the budget; estimated root-problem completeness 6/10 until proven; maximally builds on existing software rather than CORE implementation patterns. Pros: mature ledger behavior and lower build cost. Cons: Tally's account/category/budget semantics, import provenance, stable agent schemas, and skill distribution may not align; private-database adapters would be unacceptable. Biggest risk: the adapter becomes a leaky compatibility layer that cannot guarantee Tally's financial or agent contract. This remains an honest discovery candidate and is elevated to a monitored kill criterion.

SELECTION. Choose Approach A because it solves the root problem with the smallest architecture that preserves a deliberate path to Approach B, while Approach C gets a fair discovery check before custom development proceeds. The non-obvious inversion is that Tally does not need to choose web versus WhatsApp: it needs a stable finance command boundary, and the surrounding agent ecosystem owns conversation.

SCOPE SIGNALS AND ROUTING. Signals present: sensitive financial-data security; external statement and possible banking boundaries; a product-wide model exceeding five entities; cross-system flows from bank files through agents to reports; future scheduled reporting; and significant unknowns around amount/date semantics, transfers, classification, encryption, and existing-tool fit. Security and significant unknowns are independently high-impact, and six signals are present. Route to /lex:discovery LEDGER before PRD. Project-shaping choices that later deserve /lex:adr are local embedded storage, channel-neutral CLI as the public boundary, and any transition to service mode.
problem_statement: Dirk cannot reliably understand personal spend across three bank accounts at two banks because transaction facts live in separate statements and descriptions and categories are inconsistent. 5 Whys: a transaction CLI is wanted because budgets and insights need actual spend; actual spend is unreliable because it is fragmented across accounts; manual aggregation is unreliable because normalization and category corrections are not durable; those corrections are not reusable because no canonical machine-operable record exists; root cause: Tally lacks an agent-operable local ledger that preserves precise account, transaction, and spend-category facts with provenance. Journey: Dirk or an AI agent registers an account, records or queries a transaction through discoverable JSON commands, assigns or corrects its spend category, and downstream modules consume the same canonical facts.
why_now: AI agents can now maintain a structured local ledger through a self-documenting CLI, replacing delayed manual consolidation before more statement history and budget decisions accumulate.
